Title: Queen departs for Uzbekistan to attend international Islamic civilization conference
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Her Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, Queen of Malaysia, has departed for Tashkent, Uzbekistan today to attend an international conference on Islamic civilisation.
The Queen will participate in the conference titled "Scientific Tradition in Islamic Civilisation: Sources, Potentials and Challenges", scheduled to take place at the Center of Islamic Civilisation in Tashkent.
Her Majesty, who is also Universiti Teknologi Malaysia chancellor, is set to deliver a royal address before more than 200 participants, comprising scholars, thinkers and statesmen.
Higher Education Minister Datuk Seri Dr Zambry Abdul Kadir is expected to accompany the Raja Permaisuri Agong at the conference on May 5.
Earlier today, Her Majesty departed for Uzbekistan from the Royal Hangar at Senai International Airport.
Among those present to bid farewell and accompany Her Majesty were Istana Negara comptroller Tan Sri Dr Azmi Rohani and Johor Mufti Datuk Yahya Ahmad.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysians warned over dubious 'badal haj' offers
Article:
JEDDAH: Malaysians are advised not to be fooled by dubious offers of badal haj (haj by proxy) services, especially those offered at "extraordinarily" low prices compared to the actual cost of the pilgrimage.
Malaysian Consul General in Jeddah Tengku Mohd Dzaraif Raja Abdul Kadir said the cost of performing haj is high, hence any unreasonable offer is feared to not meet the prescribed pillars and obligatory haj.
"If badal haj services are offered at a cost that is too cheap, we need to be careful because it may involve things that do not meet the requirements of the pilgrimage," he told reporters after the arrival of the first group of pilgrims at the Haj Terminal of King Abdul Aziz International Airport here today.
Earlier, a total of 276 pilgrims of group KT48 via the first direct KLIA-Jeddah flight arrived at 4.40am local time.
Mohd Dzaraif explained that for domestic haj packages in Saudi Arabia itself, the minimum cost is around RM9,000 to RM10,000.
"If there is an offer below this range, it may not be logical or is dubious," he said.
He added that so far no reports have been received involving Malaysians regarding badal haj scams, but cases reported by local media involving other countries prove that is still happening.
The public is advised to verify the details and not rush to make payments to any party offering the service.
Meanwhile, he reminded pilgrims of the strict enforcement by the Saudi Arabian government ahead of the haj season, including permit checks through the Nusuk system to ensure that only those with valid visas are allowed to be in Makkah.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MCMC warns against sharing provocative 3R content online
Article:
CYBERJAYA: The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission has issued a stern warning to the public against producing or distributing online content touching on race, religion, and the royal institution (3R), particularly material that is provocative, insulting, incites hatred, or risks triggering public tension.
"The MCMC takes seriously the misuse of network services and online platforms that could harm social harmony.
"The public is advised to verify the accuracy of information before sharing it, to avoid misunderstanding or hostility," the commission said in a statement today.
The commission said it would work with the police and relevant agencies to monitor, investigate, and take action against content that violated the law.
Offenders face action under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act, which carries penalties of a fine of up to RM500,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both.
MCMC also warned that it would take action against platform providers that failed to cooperate, reminding them of their obligation to comply with the law and to prevent the misuse of their services.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man dies 2 days after Johor crash, buried beside wife and daughter
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: A Proton Persona driver involved in a crash at Km71, Jalan Johor Baru–Mersing on Friday died last night.
He had earlier been reported in critical condition.
Mohd Aiman, 20, the third child of Kamilin Alias, 56, and Rubeta Mohd, 51, confirmed the death when contacted yesterday.
He said his father (Kamilin) was pronounced dead at 10.35pm.
On Friday
it was reported that 2 women were killed in a crash involving 2 cars on the Mersing Road
heading towards this district.
In the crash, Rubeta and Nur Isyirah Syakirah were pronounced dead at the scene, while Kamilin, who was seriously injured, was taken to Sultan Ismail Hospital in Johor Bahru for treatment.
Yesterday, the couple's second child, Mohd Izat, was reported as saying his father, who was initially conscious, was later sedated due to rapid breathing and underwent stomach surgery, with bleeding also reported in the lungs.
Kamilin's remains were transported by hearse from the hospital to Taman Sri Saujana mosque this morning for funeral prayers led by Mohd Izat.
He was later laid to rest at Makam Dagang Muslim Cemetery, Kampung Makam, beside his wife Rubeta and youngest child Nur Isyirah Syakirah, who were buried in a shared grave yesterday.
More than 100 family members, relatives and friends attended the burial to pay their last respects.
On Friday, district police chief Superintendent Yusof Othman said authorities received information on the fatal crash involving a Proton Persona and a Perodua Bezza.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mental health at work to become legal duty for employers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Department of Occupational Safety and Health (DOSH) is moving to make mental health protection a statutory requirement for employers, framing psychosocial wellbeing as a legal duty under the Occupational Safety and Health Act.
The announcement was made in conjunction with the National-Level World Day for Safety and Health at Work 2026, officiated by Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan today.
DOSH said the new approach required workplaces to be adapted to meet both the physical and psychological needs of employees, with compliance expected across all sectors.
The push to strengthen mental health protection forms part of the Occupational Safety and Health Master Plan 2026–2030 (OSHMP30), which the department began implementing earlier this year.
Strategic Thrust 2 of the plan focuses on enhancing worker health and wellbeing, including mental health promotion, occupational health management, and the creation of workplaces that are safe from both physical and psychosocial hazards.
To support employers, DOSH has introduced the Guidelines on Psychosocial Risk Assessment and Management at the Workplace 2024 (PRisMA Guidelines), which provide a structured framework for identifying, assessing and managing psychosocial risks at work.
In the construction sector, DOSH is also enforcing the Occupational Safety and Health (Construction Work) (Design and Management) Regulations 2024 (CDM Regulations 2024), which adopt a life-cycle approach to risk management across all phases of construction projects.
The regulations assign clear responsibilities to all duty holders – including clients, designers and contractors – covering not only physical safety but also psychosocial factors that may contribute to workplace stress.
DOSH has also launched the CDM Regulations Guidelines 2026 Edition to further strengthen governance in the high-risk sector. It has also forged partnerships with Tun Razak Exchange City Sdn Bhd and IJM Corporation Berhad in developing OSHCDM compliance documentation under the new framework.
The event marked World Day for Safety and Health at Work, observed globally on April 28 under the International Labour Organisation.
This year's theme, "Together Ensuring a Healthy Psychosocial Work Environment", calls for collective action from the government, employers, workers and the wider community.
Jointly organised by the Human Resources Ministry, the National Council for Occupational Safety and Health, DOSH and the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso), the event also paid tribute to victims of workplace accidents and occupational diseases, while reinforcing efforts to build a stronger culture of prevention across industries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Evolving landscape of modern diplomacy
Article:
IN an era marked by great-power rivalry, technological disruption and growing global uncertainty, a fundamental question arises: does diplomacy still matter?
The answer is yes, but not in the way it once did.
Today's global landscape is defined by competing major powers, transnational challenges and rapid technological change. Issues such as climate change, pandemics, cyber threats and economic instability cannot be managed by any single state acting alone.
In this context, diplomacy is not simply relevant. It is indispensable.
Diplomacy enables states to translate national interests into international outcomes through dialogue, negotiation, and cooperation.
Unlike military or economic tools, diplomacy relies on persuasion, legitimacy and sustained engagement.
It creates space for compromise and helps prevent miscalculation, particularly in times of heightened tension.
Modern diplomacy now includes multilateral engagement, public diplomacy, digital communication and preventive mechanisms aimed at addressing crises before they escalate.
Asean, for example, illustrates how diplomacy can be institutionalised to promote dialogue, cooperation and stability.
Through platforms such as the Asean Regional Forum and the East Asia Summit, Asean provides space for both regional and major powers to engage constructively, even amid strategic competition.
Asean's approach, often referred to as the "Asean Way", emphasises consensus, non-interference and gradual confidence-building.
While sometimes criticised for being slow or cautious, this model has contributed to maintaining regional stability in a diverse and politically complex environment.
It demonstrates that diplomacy does not always rely on formal alliances or binding commitments, but can also function through sustained engagement and shared norms.
Economic diplomacy, in particular, has become a key tool for promoting trade, attracting investment and navigating global economic governance.
In an interconnected world, economic policy and foreign policy are increasingly intertwined, making diplomacy essential for managing both prosperity and security.
At the same time, diplomacy also contributes to the development and maintenance of international norms.
Through multilateral institutions and continuous engagement, states negotiate rules and principles that guide behaviour, from sovereignty and non-intervention to emerging issues such as climate governance and digital security.
These norms provide a degree of predictability and stability in an otherwise uncertain system.
Yet, despite its continued importance, diplomacy faces significant challenges in the 21st century.
FIRST
, the rise of great-power rivalry has made diplomatic engagement more complex and, at times, more fragile. Strategic competition between major powers — particularly in areas such as trade, technology, and security — has increased mistrust and reduced the space for cooperation.
In such an environment, diplomacy must operate within tighter constraints, often managing competition rather than resolving it.
SECOND
, the growing influence of non-state actors has reshaped the diplomatic landscape.
Non-governmental organisations, multinational corporations and even armed groups now play a role in international affairs, complicating traditional state-to-state diplomacy.
While these actors can support cooperation and norm-building, they can also introduce new sources of instability and contestation.
THIRD
, technological change has transformed how diplomacy is conducted. Digital platforms allow governments to communicate instantly with global audiences, increasing transparency but also exposing diplomacy to public scrutiny and real-time pressure.
While digital diplomacy enhances speed and reach, it also creates risks, including misinformation, cyber threats, and reduced flexibility in sensitive negotiations.
FOURTH
, domestic political pressures increasingly influence foreign policy decisions. Governments must balance international commitments with internal political realities, which can limit diplomatic flexibility and slow decision-making.
In many cases, domestic considerations shape how states engage externally, affecting both the pace and substance of diplomacy.
FINALLY
, there is a growing erosion of trust in international norms and institutions. Disagreements over global issues, inconsistent enforcement of rules, and geopolitical divisions have weakened confidence in multilateral systems. As trust declines, reaching consensus becomes more difficult and the effectiveness of diplomacy is reduced.
Taken together, these challenges do not diminish the importance of diplomacy. Instead, they reinforce the need for it to adapt.
Diplomacy today must be more flexible, inclusive and responsive. It must engage not only governments, but also non-state actors and global publics. It must integrate traditional negotiation with digital tools and technical expertise.
Most importantly, it must operate in an environment where cooperation and competition co-exist.
In this context, diplomacy is no longer simply about preventing conflict. It is about managing complexity.
Asean's experience offers a useful lesson. Despite operating in a region marked by diversity and external pressure, Asean has maintained its relevance by providing platforms for dialogue and by adapting its diplomatic practices to changing conditions.
Its emphasis on engagement, even in the absence of binding agreements, highlights the continued value of diplomacy as a stabilising force.
Ultimately, diplomacy remains an essential instrument of statecraft, not because it eliminates conflict, but because it provides the means to manage it.
THE WRITER IS A BRIGADIER GENERAL OF THE ROYAL MALAYSIAN AIR FORCE AND CURRENTLY ATTENDING THE NATIONAL RESILIENCE COLLEGE AT THE NATIONAL CENTRE FOR DEFENCE STUDIES (PUSPAHANAS) PUTRAJAYA
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Crowded court as 113 foreigners charged with illegal entry, overstaying
Article:
PASIR MAS: It was a packed day at the Pasir Mas Sessions Court as 113 undocumented migrants were charged with immigration offences.
All 113, charged either with illegal entry or overstaying, pleaded guilty before Sessions Court judge Zulkifli Abdullah.
Of the total, 85 were charged under Section 15(1)(c) of the Immigration Act for overstaying, while the remaining 28 were charged under Section 6(1)(c) for entering the country without valid travel documents.
The group, all men, appealed for leniency through a court interpreter.
The court fined 40 of the men RM10,000 each, in default of three months' jail. The remaining 73 were sentenced to three months' imprisonment.
The migrants comprised 86 Bangladeshis, 10 Indonesians, nine Myanmar nationals, seven Pakistanis, and one Chinese national.
They were arrested by the Immigration Department during raids at separate construction sites in Dabong, Kuala Krai.
Lawyer Nik Nur Adilla Nik Sauki represented 40 of the accused, while the others were unrepresented. Deputy public prosecutor Siti Khaidijah Khairuddin prosecuted.
Earlier, the men were brought to court in two "Black Maria" trucks and escorted into the courtroom in batches.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EAIC flags high-risk gaps in fuel subsidy crisis management
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Enforcement Agency Integrity Commission (EAIC) has identified several high-risk governance lapses that could emerge in managing an energy supply crisis arising from conflicts in the Middle East, particularly in relation to the distribution of aid and fuel subsidies.
Its chairman Tan Sri Dr Ismail Bakar said the risks increased when enforcement is carried out under urgent conditions to prevent leakages of public resources, including subsidies for RON95 petrol, diesel and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG).
He said that based on EAIC's oversight of 20 enforcement agencies, three main risk areas had been identified during crisis periods – including the smuggling of subsidised fuel, particularly at border points where enforcement officers may abuse their powers or fail to adhere to standard operating procedures (SOPs).
"For instance, enforcement officers stationed at border entry points may allow vehicles carrying subsidised fuel to pass unchecked if inspections are not carried out strictly and thoroughly, resulting in the fuel being smuggled out by irresponsible parties," he told Bernama.
Ismail said EAIC had also flagged risks of misappropriation of fuel supplies among enforcement personnel, particularly where operational allocations are diverted for personal use or benefit.
He said the commission had previously received complaints and conducted investigations into cases involving misuse of departmental fuel supplies, underscoring the need for stronger internal controls in managing operational resources.
At the same time, he stressed that effective monitoring at petrol stations and other high-risk locations was crucial in preventing subsidy leakages, including cases where individuals misuse subsidies by allowing their identity cards to be used by ineligible parties.
Commenting on the government's decision to deploy police personnel to 151 high-risk petrol stations, Ismail described the move as a proactive step that could strengthen enforcement and curb leakage of fuel subsidies.
"This also allows officers from the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry to focus on other high-risk areas such as fishermen's diesel depots, unbranded stations, illegal jetties, as well as ship-to-ship transfers and illegal bunkering activities," he said.
However, he said that the effectiveness of these measures remained at an early stage and required continuous monitoring to assess their actual impact on reducing subsidy leakages.
Ismail said EAIC had not detected any clear early signs of abuse of power or governance misconduct following the implementation of the government's intervention measures. Nevertheless, monitoring of public complaints will continue to be strengthened, given that subsidised goods remain a high-risk area during crises.
He added that coordination among enforcement agencies must be improved through a more structured approach to avoid overlapping duties and operational delays, while enhancing overall efficiency.
"Among the key measures that can be implemented is the establishment of a crisis coordination or command centre at national or ministerial level to ensure instructions are centrally coordinated and agencies carry out their responsibilities in an orderly manner," he said.
He also said clear SOPs were essential in strengthening governance during crises, including defining jurisdictions, workflows and communication channels to ensure agencies understand their roles and avoid functional overlap.
"Centralised information-sharing through digital systems or shared platforms would enable agencies to act on uniform data, reducing inconsistencies and delays in enforcement actions," he said.
"This approach can improve monitoring efficiency and facilitate quicker detection of integrity issues, especially in crisis situations requiring immediate response."
He added that appointing liaison officers or focal persons within each agency would also help improve coordination and ensure information is conveyed accurately and consistently.
Ismail said EAIC would continue proactive integrity risk assessments through monitoring and targeted investigations to identify weaknesses in enforcement systems, with a focus on preventing misconduct and strengthening integrity in government intervention policies.
He said that transparency remained key to ensuring emergency policies and targeted assistance are implemented effectively without public confusion.
"Transparency requires clear, accurate and consistent communication through official channels, with all decisions and implementation criteria reviewed and coordinated among agencies to ensure the public receives verified information," he added.
Ismail stressed that crisis communication must be coordinated through inter-agency verification to avoid conflicting statements or misinformation that could undermine public trust.
"Any weaknesses in coordination or inaccuracies in information that affect public confidence are matters of concern from a governance and integrity perspective," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fadhlina orders review of SPM Moral Education subject after complaints over difficulty
Article:
SUBANG JAYA: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has directed the Malaysian Examinations Syndicate to review and improve the Moral Education subject following complaints from the Indian community.
"When the subject of Moral Education was raised, and given the anxieties of parents and the community, I have already directed the Examinations Syndicate to review and improve the Moral Education subject," she said.
Fadlina said this in her speech at the 2026 Kalvi Yutham, an educational and motivational programme for Tamil school students, held at SJK (Tamil) Castlefield in Puchong here today.
Also in attendance were Selangor Education Department director Datin Wan Nor Ashikin Abd Kassim and Sri Murugan Centre (SMC) director Surain Kanda.
The issue was raised by Surain ahead of her address, and Fadhlina said she was grateful for the community's candour.
"I thank Surain for this, because this is exactly what we want when we talk about education.
"I cannot be a minister who acts as though I know everything — the era of the government knowing everything is over.
"This is the era of working together to continuously improve education," she said.
In his speech, Surain said his organisation had pressed for the concerns surrounding the Moral Education subject to be addressed through official channels.
"(Fadhlina) gave her promise that they will revisit this Moral Education issue seriously. We highly expect them to follow through on what they said," he said.
In a press conference after the event, Surain elaborated on the specific grievances the community had with the Moral Education subject.
The first concerned the exam paper itself.
He said Moral Education was currently a closed paper, meaning students could not take it home after the examination, leaving them with no past-year papers to study from.
Instead, he said, it should be an open paper.
The second was about the marking system. Surain said Moral Education was currently graded using 'holistic marking,' a method typically applied to language subjects, and called for it to be replaced with 'analytical marking,' as used in other subjects.
The third concerned the grading threshold for the highest band. Surain said students currently needed to score 95 and above to obtain an A+, a bar he described as unreasonably high.
Surain said the community requests for matriculation offers to Indian students be raised to 2,500 places without encroaching on the quota allocations of other communities was modest and targeted.
In response, Fadhlina reaffirmed the government's commitment to automatically offering matriculation places to students who score straight As in their SPM examinations, regardless of their ethnic background.
"Ten As — automatic (offer). For those with ten As, it is automatic, regardless of background, they are all offered places automatically.
"And this we want to defend," she said in a speech.
Fadhlina acknowledged that there were legitimate concerns within the Indian community that high-achieving students were being denied matriculation places, and said the government was actively reinforcing the policy to address this.
The minister was candid about the political difficulty of maintaining the automatic offer policy alongside the existing 90/10 matriculation quota structure, in which 90 per cent of places are reserved for Bumiputera students.
Fadhlina appealed to community leaders to understand the broader social context within which the government was operating.
"I also ask Surain and friends to understand this matter in its broader social context. It is not easy to defend, but we want to fight for this policy," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesia's Mount Semeru erupts, ash column reaches 900 metres
Article:
JAKARTA: Indonesia's Mount Semeru on the border of Lumajang and Malang regencies in East Java erupted Sunday morning, sending an ash column up to 900 metres above its summit, authorities said.
In a statement, an observation officer at the Semeru monitoring post Sigit Rian Alfian said the eruption occurred at 8am local time, with the ash plume reaching about 4,576 metres above sea level.
"The volcanic ash column lasted about 111 seconds was observed to be white to grey in colour and drifting towards the southwest with thick intensity," he said, as reported by Antara News Agency on Sunday.
Monitoring data showed that within the six-hour period from midnight to 6am, Semeru recorded 18 eruption-related tremors with amplitudes ranging from 10 to 22mm and durations between 89 and 201 seconds.
The highest peak on Java Island also recorded other seismic activity, including gas emissions, harmonic tremor, and one distant tectonic earthquake during the same period.
Authorities maintained Semeru's alert status at Level III (Alert) and warned residents against all activities within 13 kilometres of the summit in the southeast sector. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Social security for Malaysians abroad: Bill to be tabled at next Parliament sitting
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government will table amendments to the Self-Employment Social Security Act at the next Parliament sitting to expand coverage for Malaysians working abroad.
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said the move, which begins with cross-border workers in Singapore, has received cabinet approval.
"We will bring this bill to Parliament. Once it is passed, the act will be implemented immediately," he said after officiating the Labour Day Mega Carnival 2026.
Ramanan said the amendments are crucial to addressing workplace safety and health.
"Workplace safety and health is not something we can take lightly," he said.
He said the proposed amendments would complement existing Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) initiatives by extending protection to Malaysians commuting or working overseas.
"We have more than 400,000 Malaysians travelling in and out of Singapore. For them, safety protection is necessary.
"Last year alone, there were about 54 fatal accidents. These are Malaysians, and they deserve the same protection as others," he said.
Ramanan added that the scheme has been designed to prevent misuse following refinements to earlier proposals.
On Friday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim announced the proposal during the National Labour Day 2026 celebration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MyDigital ID SSO rollout for MyJPJ smooth, no glitches reported
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The rollout of the MyDigital ID Single Sign-On (SSO) integration for the MyJPJ application, enforced Friday (May 1), has proceeded smoothly with no technical disruptions reported.
MyDigital ID said in a statement that the system remained stable and is functioning as intended, enabling users to access services more easily and securely.
To date, some seven million MyJPJ users have registered and logged in using MyDigital ID, bringing the total number of MyDigital ID users to 12 million.
The agency said the SSO implementation also enhanced cybersecurity, as users need only a single verified digital identity – based on official government records – to access public services.
"This approach reduces reliance on multiple passwords, thereby lowering the risk of account breaches, digital fraud and identity misuse," it said.
Users are encouraged to download and activate the MyDigital ID application to enable SSO access to MyJPJ, and to ensure both applications are updated to their latest versions.
MyDigital ID added that it would continue to work closely with the Road Transport Department Malaysia and the National Cyber Security Agency Malaysia to ensure a secure, fast and seamless user experience, in line with efforts to strengthen the country's digital services ecosystem.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Body of 9-year-old swept away in Bandar Mahkota Cheras found near Kajang
Article:
KAJANG: The body of nine-year-old Nur Qaseh Deandra Mohd Qayyum, who fell into a drain and was swept away by strong currents in Bandar Mahkota Cheras on Friday, has been found this afternoon.
The body was discovered by search and rescue teams sweeping the Sungai Langat river near Kajang town.
The discovery was confirmed by Selangor Fire and Rescue Department Zone Four chief Wan Syariman Wan Salleh.
"The victim was discovered at 12.17pm along Sungai Langat, about 11km from the location where she was reported to have fallen near Kondominium Sri Hijau.
"The body was found by the Fire and Rescue Department's Water Rescue Team (PPDA), who conducted a search by boat along the river," he said.
Syariman added that the victim's parents identified the body at the scene.
"The remains were later sent to the Kajang Hospital forensic unit," he said.
On Friday, the 9-year-old girl
fell into a drain
while attempting to retrieve a ball before being swept away by strong currents, prompting an extensive SAR operation covering several kilometres of the river.
The Fire and Rescue Department later received an emergency call on the incident at 7.08pm.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heatwave toll: 56 cases, 2 dead despite 'low-risk' conditions
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Health Ministry has recorded 56 cases of heat-related illnesses nationwide, including two deaths from heatstroke, between Jan 1 and today.
In a statement, the ministry said the fatalities involved a two-year-old boy who was left in a vehicle and a 42-year-old man who took part in a marathon in Penang.
"Both incidents occurred when weather conditions were below Warning Level 1.
"However, the main risk factors identified were exposure to extreme heat in an enclosed environment and prolonged strenuous physical activity, which led to severe dehydration and the body's inability to regulate temperature," it said.
Of the total cases, 47 were heat exhaustion, four exertional heatstroke, four heatstroke and one heat cramp.
The ministry said its analysis found that 58 per cent of heat-related cases were linked to physical activity during hot weather.
"Nearly half of these involved sports and athletic activities such as cross-country, training sessions and marathons, while the remainder were linked to high-intensity physical training by security and operations personnel.
"In this regard, the ministry advises the public to remain vigilant and take preventive measures during hot weather," it said.
Among the measures recommended are limiting strenuous physical activity and prolonged exposure to heat. Those working in hot conditions are advised to take frequent breaks in shaded areas to cool down.
The public is also urged to drink plenty of plain water, even when not thirsty, to stay hydrated, and to use protective items such as hats, umbrellas and light-coloured, loose-fitting clothing.
High-risk groups, including infants, children, the elderly and those with chronic illnesses, should ensure adequate hydration at all times. Parents and guardians are also reminded not to leave children unattended in vehicles.
Those experiencing symptoms such as dizziness, nausea, muscle cramps or extreme fatigue should seek immediate medical attention.
"The ministry also calls on all parties, including government agencies, educational institutions and care centres, to take appropriate precautions, particularly when planning and conducting outdoor activities, to reduce the risk of heat-related illnesses.
"The public can obtain the latest updates on hot weather conditions via the Malaysian Meteorological Department website, as well as guidance on preventive measures from the ministry's website," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Adverse weather expected in KL, 8 states until 3pm — MetMalaysia
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thunderstorms, heavy rain and strong winds are expected to affect the capital and eight other states until 3 pm today, according to the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia).
In an advisory issued at 12.30pm, MetMalaysia said the affected areas in Kedah include Langkawi, while in Perak, it involves Larut, Matang and Selama, Kuala Kangsar, Kinta, Kampar, Batang Padang and Muallim.
On the East Coast, the warning covers Dungun and Kemaman in Terengganu, as well as several areas in Pahang, including Cameron Highlands, Lipis, Raub, Bentong, Kuantan, Pekan and Rompin.
The department also forecast similar weather conditions across Kuala Lumpur and parts of Selangor, namely Hulu Selangor, Klang, Gombak, Kuala Langat, Hulu Langat and Sepang.
Similar conditions are also expected in Negeri Sembilan (Jelebu, Seremban, Kuala Pilah and Rembau) and Johor (Tangkak, Segamat, Kluang, Mersing, Kulai, Kota Tinggi and Johor Bahru), as well as in Sabah involving Tawau (Lahad Datu), Sandakan (Telupid, Kinabatangan, Beluran and Sandakan) and Kudat (Pitas and Kudat). – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bandar Mahkota Cheras drain tragedy: Recovered ball renews hope in search for missing 9-year-old
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The family of a nine-year-old girl feared drowned after falling into a drain in Bandar Mahkota Cheras here remains hopeful that she will be found soon.
The girl,
Nur Qaseh Deandra Mohd Qayyum
, had fallen into the drain on Friday while attempting to retrieve a ball before being swept away by strong currents.
The girl's aunt, Fakhira Fatin Mohd Saad, 31, said the ball has been recovered and has given the family renewed hope as the search and rescue (SAR) operation entered its third day today.
"Everyone in the family hopes Qaseh will be found today. The discovery of the ball she was trying to retrieve also gives us hope," she said.
The family had been informed about the recovery of the ball on the first day of the operation.
"We are all saddened by this and Qaseh is yet to be found, but her parents remain emotionally stable. Our only hope is for Qaseh to be found soon," she said.
She described Qaseh, the third of four siblings, as a cheerful child who loved playing with her elder brother.
"The last time I saw Qaseh was on April 18 when we celebrated Hari Raya together. She is a very cheerful niece.
"Today is the third day she has been missing, and we hope the public will continue praying for her to be found soon," she said.
Operation commander and Kajang Fire and Rescue Station chief Izman Adnan said the ball was recovered on the first day of the SAR operation and brought ashore yesterday.
However, he said none of the victim's belongings have been found so far and the search is still ongoing.
As of today, the search area for the missing girl has been expanded to Sungai Langat, spanning 29km.
The
operation
covers a five-kilometre radius from where she had fallen at Sri Hijau Apartments to Sungai Long, which connects to Sungai Langat.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: More Nepalis drive electric, evading global fuel shocks
Article:
KATHMANDU: As global fuel markets reel from the Middle East war, motorists in Nepal are increasingly turning to electric vehicles, with high demand putting a strain on dealerships.
Electric microbus driver Purushottam Adhikari said he was now shuttling more passengers along the 300-kilometre (186-mile) journey between his town in Chitwan district and the capital Kathmandu.
"My profession is not affected (by the conflict)," said the 48-year-old, who drives his 18-seater Chinese-made e-van along Nepal's highways daily. "In fact, more people are choosing EVs."
Soaring global oil prices and fuel supply disruption since Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz have led to long queues at gas stations in countries like Bangladesh and Pakistan, but Nepal has largely avoided the worst of the crisis.
"One of the main reasons is the increased penetration of electric vehicles on Nepal's roads," alternative energy expert Govind Raj Pokharel told AFP.
The country of 30 million people has an estimated 50,000 EVs, still a small fraction of the total 6.2 million motor vehicles, but a figure officials expect would keep increasing.
The price of petrol in Nepal, which imports all of its gasoline, has nearly doubled since the war began in late February with US-Israeli strikes on Iran.
While transport fares have gone up, Adhikari told AFP that his prices remain unchanged at 700 rupees (US$4.60) for a one-way trip in his Joylong A6, which in turn costs US$8 for a full recharge.
A full tank for a similar diesel vehicle would have cost "more than US$66", he said.
Comfortable and cheap
An expanding network of charging stations along major highways has made long-distance electric travel increasingly viable.
Boarding Adhikari's van, 20-year-old Susmita Bishowkarma said she prefers to travel in EVs because they are "environmentally friendly... comfortable and comparatively cheap."
Buoyed by a surge in hydropower generation and a greener electric grid than neighbouring India and Bangladesh, Nepal has emerged as one of the world's fastest adopters of EVs.
The Himalayan nation imported more than 13,500 EVs between mid-2024 and mid-2025 – double that of petrol vehicles and a sharp rise from just seven a decade ago, government data shows.
Fume-free taxis and cars manufactured in China are a common sight in Kathmandu, with traffic officials estimating that up to 60 percent of microbuses entering the city from key routes are now electric.
Customs department spokesman Kishor Bartaula said the number of EVs would rise further with hundreds awaiting clearance at Nepal's ports.
In line with its clean energy policy, the government is seeking to replace some 10,000 vehicles damaged during anti-corruption protests last year with EVs, finance ministry spokesman Amrit Lamsal told AFP.
Demand surge
Concerns that ongoing conflict in the Middle East could prolong global fuel uncertainty are also influencing consumer behaviour.
Shraban Bhattari, 49, who recently bought a BYD Atto-2, said the Chinese-made car is saving him daily fuel expenses.
"I no longer need to go to the petrol pump," he said.
In April the government approved a legal framework to allow people to convert their petrol and diesel vehicles into electric ones, known as "retrofitting."
But EV dealers told AFP they were struggling to meet demand.
"It is getting challenging to meet the demand," said Ritima Pandey, customer relations officer at Venture Motors.
"As the price of diesel has gone up, many people are coming to trade in fuel vehicles for EV vans."
Schools and colleges are also exploring electric fleet purchases, dealer Dinesh Raj Pandeya said, signalling a shift beyond individual commuters.
Energy expert Pokharel urged policymakers to build on the momentum by encouraging domestic EV manufacturing and assembly.
"This will give us a long-term solution," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Visit Chinese, Tamil schools too, Fadhlina tells education officials
Article:
SUBANG JAYA: Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek has told ministry officials to visit vernacular schools, not just national schools, saying it reflects the ministry's commitment to education for all.
"Chinese children are our children. Indian children are our children. Malay children are our children.
"When visiting schools, do not visit only national schools. SJKCs (Chinese schools) are our schools. SJKTs (Tamil schools) are our schools."
Fadhlina said this in her speech at the 2026 Kalvi Yutham, an educational and motivational programme for Tamil school students.
This year's event, attended by Selangor Education Department director Datin Wan Nor Ashikin Abd Kassim and Sri Murugan Centre director Surain Kanda, was held at Sekolah Jenis Kebangsaan (Tamil) Castlefield in Puchong.
Speaking on sentiments against vernacular education by some quarters, Fadhlina assured that these schools would not be closed.
She said SJKCs and SJKTs are enshrined in the Education Act.
Separately, Fadhlina said she will meet the SJKT Headmasters' Association twice a year to resolve outstanding issues.
She added that maintenance and relocation matters must be addressed while the current government still holds its mandate.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] PM: Bumiputera-empowerment agenda bolder than ever under Madani govt
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has defended the Madani government's Bumiputera empowerment agenda, saying it has been pursued more "assertively, firmly and boldly" than before.
Dismissing claims by certain quarters, Anwar said his administration has not supported elites at the expense of Bumiputera interests.
"The country will not deviate from the principle of 'justice as fairness', which ensures space and fairness, especially for marginalised and neglected communities.
"We must understand that political stability is impossible without safeguarding the majority population, which is generally poorer," he said in a speech at Mara's 60th anniversary celebration.
Anwar said the 90 per cent Bumiputera quota for matriculation remains, even as the government has decided that any student, regardless of background, who obtains 10As will be offered a place.
"As such, in any debate, one should not selectively highlight only one aspect. For instance, some academics, including professors, highlight the 90 per cent allocation to Bumiputera students but fail to note that this applies only to matriculation programmes.
"Previously, matriculation was 100 per cent reserved for Bumiputera students, but 10 per cent has since been allocated, particularly for underprivileged and marginalised non-Malay students.
"At the same time, there are also some Malays who argue that recognising all students who achieve 10As is unfair.
"This is the kind of polemic often described as a zero-sum game, and it will persist."
Anwar added that he has entrusted Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi to ensure Bumiputera programmes are prioritised and presented at the Bumiputera Development Council, which he chairs.
"If there are policy matters, they are then brought to Cabinet meetings. It would not be easy for these issues to be set aside when they are tabled by the prime minister or deputy prime minister at the cabinet level.
"Cabinet meetings are not, as some have assumed, influenced or intimidated by the so-called 'ghosts' of other parties. That does not happen."
On education, Anwar said Mara's role has been further strengthened with an increased allocation of RM43 billion, benefiting more than 1.8 million students.
"This includes programmes such as Ulul Albab, which demonstrate that Bumiputera students are capable of excelling in a comprehensive education system that balances modern knowledge with religious values," he said.
From an economic perspective, Anwar highlighted a positive trend in Bumiputera household income, which rose by more than 10 per cent, from about RM6,300 in 2022 to RM7,000 in 2024, while acknowledging that disparities still exist and require continued attention.
On efforts to eradicate hardcore poverty, he said the government has also increased development allocations for states, including those not aligned with the federal government, through significant budget increases.
Also present were Zahid, Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar, and Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Vietnam January-April coffee exports rise 16pct to 810,000 tonnes
Article:
HANOI: Vietnam exported 810,000 metric tonnes of coffee in the first four months of 2026, up 15.8 per cent from the same period a year earlier, government data showed on Sunday.
Export revenue over the period fell 7 per cent to US$3.69 billion, the statistics office said in a report.
In April alone, Vietnam exported 220,000 tonnes of beans, with a value of US$936 million.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar wants Mara to be Bumiputera development one-stop centre
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today said Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) should serve as a central economic umbrella and a one-stop centre for Bumiputera development.
He said under this expanded role, Mara would be entrusted with coordinating Bumiputera development efforts across all relevant agencies in the country.
"This is a new responsibility so that all agencies under the Economy Ministry, together with other ministries, when it comes to a one-stop centre for Bumiputera development, we can shift that role and entrust it to Mara," he said in his speech at the Mara 60th anniversary celebration.
Also present were Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, Deputy Rural and Regional Development Minister Datuk Rubiah Wang, Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar, and Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki.
Meanwhile, Anwar also directed that the draft amendments to the Mara Act be completed and submitted within three months.
"I hope the draft amendments to the Mara Act, which the deputy prime minister said would be completed by year-end, will not need to wait.
These are all experts, all of them are skilled. Why do you want to wait for months? Give me the draft within three months," he said.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Car vaults over divider, crashes into oncoming traffic [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A car
lost control and vaulted across a road divider
, crashing into two oncoming vehicles in a high-speed collision captured on dashcam.
The footage, posted by @UpdateInfo on X on May 1, shows the three-vehicle wreck occurring on a multi-lane road late at night.
The video begins with a quiet road before a car from the opposite direction suddenly hurtles over the median, slamming directly into an oncoming vehicle in the right lane.
The force of the impact sent the car airborne, where it struck a second vehicle following closely behind from where the dashcam was filming.
The car had flipped sideways before landing upright in front of the second vehicle it hit, as seen in the clip.
In the aftermath, other motorists and passersby were seen stopping to assist as traffic ground to a standstill.
It remains unclear exactly where the incident took place or if there were any casualties.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Water Festival boosts SME activity amid tough operating climate
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Water Festival Bukit Bintang 2026 has delivered meaningful economic spillovers for small and medium enterprises (SMEs), reinforcing the role of large-scale tourism events as catalysts for business activity amid a challenging operating environment.
In a statement, SME Association of Malaysia president Dr Chin Chee Seong said large-scale, vibrant tourism events such as this are more than cultural celebrations, describing them as important economic catalysts at a time when SMEs are facing slower growth, rising costs and an uncertain business environment.
The three-day urban lifestyle event, held from April 30 to May 2 in Bukit Bintang under the Visit Malaysia 2026 campaign, transformed the city centre into an immersive music and cultural experience featuring international and local DJs, food bazaars, artisan markets and cultural showcases.
Chin said Bukit Bintang, as a key tourism and commercial hub, provides a strategic platform to highlight Malaysia's diversity, creativity and hospitality.
He said the event, fully supported by the Tourism, Arts and Culture Ministry, not only increases visitor traffic and boosts tourist spending but also generates immediate business opportunities for SMEs across retail, food and beverage, hospitality, transport and services.
"Such initiatives generate strong multiplier effects across the SME ecosystem. Increased visitor traffic translates into higher sales, improved cash flow and strengthened business sentiment, factors that are critically needed as SMEs continue to navigate cost pressures, demand volatility and global economic headwinds," he said.
Chin added that beyond economic impact, the water festival also strengthens cultural appreciation and social cohesion by bringing together local and international visitors, reinforcing Malaysia's identity as a multicultural nation.
He said such initiatives help enhance Malaysia's global positioning as a vibrant and welcoming tourism destination, particularly amid growing regional competition for international visitors.
The association urged the government to continue expanding and promoting similar high-impact tourism events, saying they have the potential to become signature national attractions that deliver long-term and inclusive benefits to local businesses.
It also called for closer collaboration between government agencies, industry players and SME stakeholders to ensure wider participation and access to opportunities generated by such programmes.
Chin said the water festival reflects the right direction, where culture, tourism and economic development converge to create meaningful impact for both businesses and society.
"The SME Association of Malaysia looks forward to supporting more initiatives of this nature that uplift SMEs, strengthen the national economy and celebrate Malaysia's unity in diversity," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Latecomer Japan eyes slice of rising global defence spending
Article:
TOKYO: Long-pacifist Japan has shed its self-imposed ban on lethal weaponry exports, gunning for a prominent place in the global defence trade – a challenging feat that analysts say could take years to achieve.
Under Japan's once-strict stance adopted following its World War II surrender and the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, it has generally restricted arms exports in recent times to non-lethal categories like rescue, transport and surveillance.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's government scrapped these constraints last month, allowing firms to supply lethal weaponry to any of the 17 countries where Japan has defence cooperation agreements.
Prohibitions on sales to nations at war remain but can be circumvented under special circumstances.
Five Japanese firms, including Mitsubishi Heavy Industries and Kawasaki Heavy Industries, are already in the top 100 global defence companies, according to the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI).
But they have largely lived off domestic demand from Japan's military, often in cooperation with US defence firms.
Analysts say a focus on high-tech sectors could help the transition, but caution that it could take years for defence exports to become a big contributor to economic growth, hampered by capacity and workforce shortages.
Bloomberg Intelligence analyst Ian Ma said it was a "transition from a domestic, ministry-led procurement model to a normal practice which companies could drive business opportunities just like other global companies are doing."
He added that as newcomers on the global market, Japanese firms should focus on "higher-tech niches" like naval and propulsion systems, advanced missiles, sensors and electronics.
No 'immediate impact'
With conflicts raging around the world, the international defence market is huge and fast growing, soaring by 41 percent between 2016 and 2025 to almost $3 trillion, according to SIPRI.
While Japanese firms may only export to 17 countries, those include some of the world's biggest defence spenders like the United States, Germany, India and Britain.
Evolutions in technology and the way wars are fought – notably with drones – are also making the global arms market more diffuse, which could benefit Japan, according to analysts at the Stimson Center.
Along with a steep decline in Russian arms exports, Washington's "increasingly unpredictable, extractive, and at times confrontational approach" has also pushed importers to seek alternatives to the United States, the Stimson Center said in a research note.
"Though the trend remains nascent, for Japan, the wandering eyes of traditional US defence partners could add to the demand for the sorts of sophisticated capabilities Tokyo is well-prepared to offer."
Even before the new changes, Japan has been no slouch.
Last year Mitsubishi Heavy Industries won a landmark order for 11 warships from the Australian navy.
Japan is also developing a new fighter jet with Britain and Italy, and countries in Southeast Asia are reportedly interested in acquiring used Japanese submarines and warships.
But Mitsubishi Electric, which supplied a radar system to the Philippines and may soon make air-to-air missiles with US partners, told AFP that the new rules would not have a "significant immediate impact on our business."
Rival IHI said that the changes "will not lead to an immediate acceleration of our defence equipment business", but "will have a significant impact on creating a market environment that accelerates international cooperation."
'Homework'
Ma cautioned that Japan has "homework" to do, including on production capacity, skilled labour, second- and third-tier suppliers, certification, testing, maintenance support and the ability to "deliver on time."
Beyond the economic stakes, deepening security ties is also a strategic necessity for Japan, said Heigo Sato, a professor at Takushoku University, in a region where China is flexing its muscles and home to nuclear-armed North Korea.
"When it comes to defence industry cooperation, Japan is by no means a country with the world's most advanced weaponry; therefore, we must actively work to build relationships with other nations," Sato told AFP.
The Japanese public is not at all comfortable with the new strategy, with 55 percent of respondents in a recent Nikkei poll saying that they were opposed to the expansion of arms exports.
Days after Takaichi announced the new rules, dozens of protestors rallied in Tokyo.
In World War II, "Japan committed acts of aggression, and in turn suffered enormous damage from the atomic bombs," demonstrator Yura Suzuike told AFP.
Japan's pacifist constitution that followed had been drafted "with the resolve that we must never again wage war or kill people", she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Honda Accord driver detained over dangerous overtaking video
Article:
PONTIAN: Police have detained the driver of a Honda Accord who went viral for dangerous driving on Friday after overtaking several vehicles along a double line.
District police chief Superintendent Hadzrat Hussein Mion Hussain said authorities identified the driver based on information received.
This followed a video recording of the incident, believed to have been taken near the slip road at the BHP and Shell petrol station junction at Km84 of Jalan Pontian–Batu Pahat.
"The driver, a 47-year-old local man, was traced during a roadblock in the district at 12 noon yesterday.
"Initial investigations found the suspect admitted to driving a silver Honda Accord along a double line and overtaking several vehicles at the location," he said.
He added that the suspect is unemployed, tested negative for drugs and has no prior criminal record.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia key EU partner, ties on upward trajectory
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is a key partner to the European Union (EU), with relations on an upward trajectory, driven by ongoing negotiations on the Malaysia-EU Free Trade Agreement (MEUFTA), said the EU Ambassador to Malaysia, Rafael Daerr.
According to him, the EU and Malaysia are like-minded in many ways, including as vibrant democracies and trade-oriented market economies that strongly rely on and support international law and a rules-based multilateral framework.
"Given broader geopolitical and geoeconomic developments and the resulting shocks, it is ever more important to strengthen resilience, in particular by diversifying supply chains and strengthening those trade partnerships that are both sustainable and mutually beneficial," he told Bernama in an interview held in conjunction with the upcoming Europe Day celebrations on May 9.
Daerr said the EU, as the world's largest single market representing roughly 16 per cent of the world economy, with nearly 450 million consumers and high purchasing power, is now Malaysia's fourth-largest trading partner and, cumulatively and historically, one of the biggest investors in the country.
He added that the EU has created over 150,000 jobs in Malaysia in the last two decades alone.
"Our economies are strongly interconnected and, judging by statistics and the positive trade balance, the potential for increase is comparatively higher with the EU than with other countries or regions in the world, alongside the similarly underdeveloped intra-Asean trade," he said, noting that Malaysia is currently EU's 20th-biggest goods trading partner.
Drawing similarities between Malaysia and EU member states in terms of population and economic size, the ambassador highlighted the importance of working together on the world stage.
"Just imagine for a moment that Malaysia were to become an EU Member State: it would be the sixth-biggest in terms of population size and currently the 11th-biggest when it comes to the size of its economy.
"This being said, for such middle-sized countries, when it comes to facing common challenges on the global stage, there is no viable alternative but to cooperate and join forces to defend our interests," he said, noting that Malaysia is a very important voice in many fora, including Asean and the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation (OIC).
Daerr said, on a personal note, that he considers it rewarding and an honour to represent the EU in a country like Malaysia, as it is a bilateral partnership that matters and has significant potential for further development.
Asked about the MEUFTA, Daerr said both sides are committed to reaching an ambitious and comprehensive agreement, and that good progress has been made.
"We have had three rounds of negotiations in the past 10 months. The fourth round is scheduled to take place from June 8 to 12 in Kuala Lumpur, with the EU team travelling here," he said, adding that the goal is to conclude negotiations before the 50th anniversary of Asean-EU relations in the final quarter of 2027.
"Our intention is to increase overall trade and investment. Seeing the strong political ambition on both sides at the highest level and the dedication of the negotiating teams on both sides, I am very optimistic that we will reach an agreement that will provide substantial benefits for both sides," Daerr said.
In view of the Europe Day reception in Malaysia, to be held on May 11, the ambassador highlighted it as an opportunity to reaffirm and celebrate the importance of cooperation and reliable, predictable partnerships.
He said the reception will bring together EU member states and partners from government and other institutions, civil society, as well as the international and business community, to thank them for their contributions to strengthening the EU-Malaysia partnership.
Daerr said that Europe Day is a joint celebration by all 27 EU member states, of which 17 have embassies in Malaysia, while 10 have envoys accredited to Malaysia from other countries.
"As 'Team Europe', we are thrilled to work in and with Malaysia, especially given that the relationship has significant potential to be further amplified in many areas, starting with trade and mutual investment, including in key areas such as semiconductors, green and other future technologies, but also beyond the economic sphere," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
